title: URIs, URL Resolution, Origins, DNS, and Destination Identity · Browser and Node Client Systems · Mastery Check
summary: A cross-runtime release reviewer evaluating a changed provider contract must defend independent decisions across fresh cases, including one ambiguous failure and one changed boundary for uris, url resolution, origins, dns, and destination identity, producing destination-resolution and origin contract. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch destination-resolution and origin contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit…
objectives: Parse scheme, authority, host, port, path, query, and fragment and resolve relative references against the correct base. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Encode path segments and query names or values at the correct structural layer without double encoding. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Compare origins from scheme, host, and effective port while separating URI authority, Host, and connection address. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Destination Validation
steps.instruction:
quiz case — universal Fetch release candidate receives base https://api2.example.test/v1/items/ and reference ../reports?q=a%2Fb. Resolve it structurally, compare origin and authority, trace two DNS answers, and reject any connected destination outside the approved range. Competency probe: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Destination Validation.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Destination Validation, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Destination Validation precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Destination Validation, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: universal Fetch release candidate receives base https://api4.example.test/v1/items/ and reference ../reports?q=a%2Fb. Resolve it structurally, compare origin and authority, trace two DNS answers, and reject any connected destination outside the approved range. Competency probe: Canonicalize and validate schemes, hosts, IP literals, ports, IDNs, and resolved destinations before granting network authority. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Destination Validation, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A regular expression over the original URL text is sufficient destination security. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
